title: Dog Swelling Between Toes with Pus: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Noticed a painful lump or pus between your dog's toes? Learn about interdigital cysts, urgency levels, and how to help your vet with a triage photo.
content_html:
What is the Swelling Between My Dog's Toes?

It is very common to feel worried when you notice a red, painful lump leaking pus between your dog's toes. This condition is most frequently an interdigital furuncle, often referred to as an interdigital cyst. These are deep-seated bacterial infections of the hair follicles that cause localized inflammation and abscesses. Other possibilities include a foreign body, such as a grass seed or splinter, or a reaction to an insect bite or sting.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. While a swollen toe with pus is rarely a life-threatening emergency, it is significantly painful for your dog and can lead to a systemic infection if left untreated. You should aim to schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours. If your dog is unable to walk, has a high fever, or the swelling is rapidly spreading up the limb, seek immediate veterinary care.

Why a Photo Helps with Triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of the affected paw can be a vital tool for your veterinary team. High-quality images allow a professional to see the exact location, the type of discharge, and the severity of the inflammation. This visual information helps the clinic determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen and can provide a baseline to track whether the swelling is improving or worsening after treatment begins.

faqs.question: Can I squeeze or pop the lump at home?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to pop or squeeze a swelling between the toes. Doing so can force the infection deeper into the surrounding tissues, causing more pain and potentially leading to a much more serious infection.

faqs.question: What can I do to keep my dog comfortable until the vet visit?
faqs.answer: You can perform a warm soak using Epsom salts (if your dog allows it) for 5-10 minutes to soothe the area. Most importantly, prevent your dog from licking the site by using an Elizabethan collar (the 'cone of shame'), as licking introduces more bacteria and slows healing.

faqs.question: Will my dog need antibiotics?
faqs.answer: In many cases involving pus, a veterinarian will prescribe a course of antibiotics. They may also recommend anti-inflammatory medication to reduce swelling and pain, and in some cases, a procedure to check for hidden foreign bodies like grass seeds.
